name: Texas Muslim Womens Foundation Inc
mission: Founded in 2005, Texas Muslim Women's Foundation (TMWF) aims to empower, promote and support all women and their families, with a special focus on North Texas. We are an equal opportunity provider. We engage all members of the community – women, men, seniors, families and youth through our various programs. We provide social and family violence services that foster peace and well-being in the home through our Peace in the Home Services and Peaceful Oasis Shelter. In addition, TMWF also arranges leadership skills development for Muslim youth to inspire the next generation to build a promising future for our society and educational programs for Muslim and non-Muslim community members to learn about topics essential to individual and community welfare.
theoryOfChange: The Texas Muslim Women's Foundation (TMWF) has a published report titled "From the Roots of Trauma to the Flowering of Trauma-Informed Care." This document details TMWF's four-year project (starting in 2014) to design and implement culturally-sensitive trauma-informed programming for domestic violence survivors and their children. The report charts their process of becoming a trauma-informed agency, which involved learning about types of trauma and trauma-informed care, assessing and enhancing existing culturally-sensitive practices, training staff, and collaborating with researchers to document and build evidence-based practice. TMWF articulates a model that integrates culturally-sensitive services and trauma-informed advocacy, which they invite others to learn from and replicate.

strengths.detail: The organization holds an <cite id="11">overall score of 98.98.66666666666667/100 from Charity Navigator</cite>, including perfect 100/100 marks for both Culture and Leadership.
TMWF demonstrates remarkable fiscal discipline, requiring only <cite id="12">$0.04 to raise every $1</cite>, ensuring more funds are available for direct services.
In 2024, the foundation successfully served <cite id="13">137 emergency shelter residents</cite> and <cite id="14">216 non-residential domestic violence clients</cite>.

strengths_deep_dive: TMWF's operational strength lies in its specialized focus on domestic violence within the Muslim community, a historically underserved space. Their financial health is robust, characterized by <cite id="15">3.7 months of working capital</cite>, which Charity Navigator classifies as resilient. This stability supports a diverse range of programs, from <cite id="16">Senior Permanent Supportive Housing</cite> to youth leadership, all while maintaining a <cite id="17">100% independent board</cite> to ensure objective oversight and governance.
rich_narrative.summary: The Texas Muslim Women's Foundation (TMWF) is a specialized social service provider dedicated to empowering women and families through culturally sensitive support, with a primary focus on domestic violence intervention. Founded in <cite id="1">2005</cite>, the organization has grown into a vital resource in North Texas, offering a comprehensive continuum of care that includes <cite id="2">emergency shelters, transitional housing, and rapid re-housing</cite>. In the 2023 fiscal year, the foundation reported <cite id="3">total revenue of $4,665,216</cite>, reflecting a significant <cite id="4">3-year revenue CAGR of 17.3%</cite>. TMWF operates with high financial efficiency, maintaining a <cite id="5">program expense ratio of 86.0%</cite> while spending only <cite id="6">$0.04 per $1 raised</cite> in fundraising costs. Their impact is quantifiable through direct service delivery; for instance, in 2023, they provided housing for <cite id="7">186 survivors in shelters</cite> and supported <cite id="8">204 survivors through transitional or rapid re-housing programs</cite>. Beyond housing, the foundation addresses the root causes of instability through <cite id="9">social services, senior supportive housing, and the 'Food 4 Good' program</cite>. As a <cite id="10">zakat-eligible organization</cite>, TMWF provides a critical avenue for Muslim donors to support vulnerable populations including the poor (fuqara) and the needy (masakin) within a faith-aligned framework.
